sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Cyanide is a rapidly acting and potentially fatal chemical that inhibits cellular respiration by blocking the mitochondrial enzyme cytochrome c oxidase. This leads to histotoxic hypoxia, where cells cannot utilise oxygen, despite its adequate supply. The most likely situation on a MinRes site to result in cyanide toxicity will be from a fire (up to and including 2025). Cyanide is produced from melting plastics and those enclosed in fires are at considerable risk.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Headache, confusion, altered mental state
- Tachycardia, hyperpnoea & hypertension in early stages, bradycardia, apnoea & hypotension in late stages
- Chest pain, cardiovascular collapse, arrythmia
- Nausea and/or vomiting
- Mydriasis (dilated and unreactive pupils)
- Metabolic acidosis
- Seizure, coma, death

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Gas detection must take place before entering any area with a suspected hazardous atmosphere
- Investigating why/how the exposure occurred is important to rule out self-harm
- Co-exposure with carbon monoxide is a major risk in smoke inhalation from enclosed-space fires
- A bitter almond smell may be absent and is not reliable diagnostically
- All suspected cyanide exposures require off-site medical review and management

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey
- Offer continuous support and reassurance if applicable
- If non-life-threatening skin or hair exposure has occurred, remove contaminated clothing and wash exposed skin with soap and water
- Position the patient appropriately
- Give high-flow oxygen immediately and continue it until handover or toxicology-directed change in management
- Record vital signs, complete focused secondary survey, and consider concurrent smoke inhalation or carbon monoxide exposure
- Contact Poisons Information on 131126
- Have a low threshold for calling the on-call doctor for all cases

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Consider advanced airway
- Provide assisted ventilation if required
- Apply cardiac monitoring and obtain 12 lead ECG
- Consider IV access
- Consider blood testing or lactate measurement where applicable
- Consider fluids if indicated
- Consider antiemetic therapy
- Consider hydroxocobalamin in consultation with the on-call doctor, especially for high-risk smoke inhalation with altered mental state, hypotension, or cardiovascular collapse
- Consider sodium bicarbonate for severe metabolic acidosis in consultation with the on-call doctor

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- Caution is advised if the cyanide compound is acetonitrile (found in industry), life- threatening toxicity can be delayed up to 24 hours later
- Patients need to be tested (urine sample for thiocyanate) for chronic cyanide toxicity 2 -3 days after exposure so arrangements for this should be made
- Always offer engagement with Mind Matters or EAP if applicable
